bugs.description: Single‑cell matrix handling is incorrect – the algorithm fails to count the trivial staircase consisting of the only cell after a lock/unlock operation.
Off‑by‑one error when accessing neighbours – the code accesses grid[x][y] instead of grid[x-1][y-1], causing IndexError for cells on the bottom or right border.
bugs.actual_behavior: IndexError: list index out of range

bugs.description: Double counting of staircases that can be represented by both pattern 1 and pattern 2 – the implementation adds the same path twice, inflating the total count.
Performance regression for maximum constraints – the algorithm recomputes the entire count from scratch after each query, leading to O(q·n·m) time which exceeds the time limit for n,m=1000 and q=10⁴.
Incorrect handling of locked cells when they appear in the middle of a potential staircase – the algorithm only checks the start and end cells, allowing paths that pass through locked cells.

bugs.description: Single‑cell matrix handling is incorrect – the algorithm fails to count the trivial staircase consisting of the only cell after a lock/unlock operation.
Off‑by‑one error when accessing neighbours – the code accesses grid[x][y] instead of grid[x-1][y-1], causing IndexError for cells on the bottom or right border.
bugs.actual_behavior: IndexError: list index out of range

bugs.description: Double counting of staircases that can be represented by both pattern 1 and pattern 2 – the implementation adds the same path twice, inflating the total count.
Performance regression for maximum constraints – the algorithm recomputes the entire count from scratch after each query, leading to O(q·n·m) time which exceeds the time limit for n,m=1000 and q=10⁴.

bugs.bug_description: Single‑cell matrix handling is incorrect – the algorithm fails to count the trivial staircase consisting of the only cell after a lock/unlock operation.
bugs.reasoning: The implementation initializes the grid with all cells free and then updates counts based on neighbour relationships. In a 1×1 grid there are no neighbours, but the code does not contain a special case to handle this minimal size. Consequently the count of staircases is never updated to reflect the single‑cell path when the cell is free, leading to the observed discrepancy (always returning 1 instead of toggling between 0 and 1).

bugs.bug_description: Off‑by‑one error when accessing neighbours – the code accesses grid[x][y] instead of grid[x-1][y-1], causing IndexError for cells on the bottom or right border.
bugs.reasoning: The input coordinates are 1‑based, but the grid is a 0‑based Python list. The snippet shows direct use of the coordinates (e.g., grid[x][y]) without subtracting one. This leads to out‑of‑range accesses for any cell on the last row or column, as demonstrated by the IndexError on the query (2,2) in a 2×2 matrix.

bugs.bug_description: Double counting of staircases that can be represented by both pattern 1 and pattern 2 – the implementation adds the same path twice, inflating the total count.
bugs.reasoning: The code enumerates staircases by iterating over both pattern types independently and adds the count for each without checking for overlap. Certain paths (e.g., those of length 1 or symmetric shapes) satisfy both patterns, so they are counted twice, which matches the observed off‑by‑one error in the test case (reported 14 instead of 13).

bugs.bug_description: Performance regression for maximum constraints – the algorithm recomputes the entire count from scratch after each query, leading to O(q·n·m) time which exceeds the time limit for n,m=1000 and q=10⁴.
bugs.reasoning: The function count_staircases rebuilds the full grid and iterates over all cells for every query (the provided snippet shows a full‑grid initialization and no incremental update logic). This results in a runtime proportional to n·m per query, which is far too slow for the worst‑case limits and causes a timeout on the large test.

bugs.bug_description: Incorrect handling of locked cells when they appear in the middle of a potential staircase – the algorithm only checks the start and end cells, allowing paths that pass through locked cells.
bugs.reasoning: The counting routine verifies that the first and last cells of a candidate staircase are free but does not validate the intermediate cells. Consequently a path that traverses a locked cell in the middle is still counted, leading to an over‑count (the second query in the example should reduce the total from 25 to 24, but the program leaves it at 25).

bugs.description: Single‑cell matrix handling is incorrect – the algorithm fails to count the trivial staircase consisting of the only cell after a lock/unlock operation.
bugs.fix: Add a special case for n = 1 and m = 1 (or more generally when the grid contains only one free cell). The total number of staircases should be 1 when the cell is free and 0 when it is locked. Update the count after each toggle accordingly.
bugs.reasoning: The implementation never updates the count for the solitary cell because it only looks at neighbour relationships. Consequently the answer stays at 1 regardless of the cell state, contradicting the expected toggle between 0 and 1.

bugs.description: Off‑by‑one error when accessing neighbours – the code accesses grid[x][y] instead of grid[x‑1][y‑1], causing IndexError for cells on the bottom or right border.
bugs.fix: Convert input coordinates from 1‑based to 0‑based before using them to index the Python list, e.g. `x -= 1; y -= 1`. Apply the same conversion for all neighbour checks.
bugs.reasoning: The snippet uses the raw coordinates directly, which exceeds the valid index range for the last row/column, leading to a runtime crash.

bugs.description: Double counting of staircases that can be represented by both pattern 1 and pattern 2 – the implementation adds the same path twice, inflating the total count.
bugs.fix: Count each staircase only once. One approach is to enumerate only one of the two patterns (e.g., always start with a right move) and treat the opposite pattern as the same path reversed, or keep a set of visited cell sequences to deduplicate.
bugs.reasoning: The code iterates over both patterns independently and adds their contributions without checking for overlap, so paths that satisfy both patterns (including length‑1 paths) are counted twice.

bugs.description: Performance regression for maximum constraints – the algorithm recomputes the entire count from scratch after each query, leading to O(q·n·m) time which exceeds the time limit for n,m = 1000 and q = 10⁴.
bugs.fix: Maintain an incremental data structure. For each cell keep the length of the longest alternating right‑down run in both directions and update only the cells whose neighbourhood changed (O(1) or O(log n) per query). The total number of staircases can be updated by adding/subtracting the contributions of the affected cells.
bugs.reasoning: The provided function rebuilds the whole grid and scans all cells for every query, which is far too slow for the worst‑case limits and results in a timeout.

bugs.description: Incorrect handling of locked cells when they appear in the middle of a potential staircase – the algorithm only checks the start and end cells, allowing paths that pass through locked cells.
bugs.fix: When evaluating a candidate staircase, verify that every cell along the path is free. This can be done by pre‑computing prefix sums for each row/column or by extending the DP that stores the maximal alternating run length, ensuring that a locked cell breaks the run.
bugs.reasoning: The counting routine validates only the first and last cells of a staircase, so a locked cell in the interior does not prevent the path from being counted, leading to an over‑count.
